  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Migration Pattern Analysis with Data Integration

Import, clean and merge the datasets.

files = list.files(
  "migration",
  pattern = "\\.xls$",
  full.names = TRUE,
  ignore.case = TRUE
)

read_migration_file = function(file_path) {
  read_excel(
    file_path,
    skip = 8,
    col_names = c(
      "dest_state_fips",
      "dest_county_fips",
      "orig_state_fips",
      "orig_county_fips",
      "state_abbr",
      "flow_description",
      "returns",
      "exemptions",
      "income_thousands"
    ),
    col_types = rep("text", 9)
  ) |>
    mutate(
      returns = parse_number(returns),
      exemptions = parse_number(exemptions),
      income_thousands = parse_number(income_thousands)
    )
}

migration_raw = map_dfr(files, read_migration_file) |> 
  arrange(dest_state_fips, dest_county_fips, orig_state_fips, orig_county_fips)

1

Identify the county pairs with the most interactions (by number of exemptions).

Method

Before analyzing migration patterns, I removed rows that did not represent meaningful county-to-county migration. Specifically, I excluded summary rows where orig_state_fips > 56, since these rows correspond to aggregated categories rather than actual U.S. counties. I also removed rows where the origin and destination counties were the same, because this project focuses on migration between counties rather than within-county records.

To identify the county pairs with the most interactions, I first created standardized county identifiers by concatenating state and county FIPS codes for both origin and destination counties. Because county-to-county migration is directional in the raw data, I reordered each pair using pmin() and pmax() so that flows between the same two counties would be treated as one undirected county pair. I then aggregated the number of exemptions across each pair and ranked the pairs from highest to lowest interaction.

To make the output more interpretable, I sourced an external county FIPS crosswalk file and used it to match county names to the county FIPS codes in the migration dataset. I then joined this lookup table back to the ranked county pairs so that the final results included both FIPS codes and county names.

# get rid of the summary rows and non-migration rows
migration_df = migration_raw |> 
  filter(
    orig_state_fips <= 56,
    !(dest_state_fips == orig_state_fips & dest_county_fips == orig_county_fips)
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    # create unique FIPS codes for each county
    dest_fips = paste0(dest_state_fips, dest_county_fips),
    orig_fips = paste0(orig_state_fips, orig_county_fips))

county_pairs = migration_df |>
  mutate(
    # create county pairs
    pair_1_fips = pmin(orig_fips, dest_fips),
    pair_2_fips = pmax(orig_fips, dest_fips)
  ) |>
  # sum exemptions for each county pair
  group_by(pair_1_fips, pair_2_fips) |>
  summarise(
    total_exemptions = sum(exemptions, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_exemptions))
# create a county name lookup table
county_lookup = read_csv("County FIPS codes.csv") |>
  transmute(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(`County FIPS Code`), width = 5, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    state_fips = str_pad(as.character(`State FIPS code`), width = 2, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    county_state = str_replace(County, ",.*$", "") |> str_c(", ", State)
  ) |> 
  janitor::clean_names()

# join county names to each county pair
top_pairs_named = county_pairs |>
  left_join(
    county_lookup |>
      rename(
        pair_1_fips = county_fips,
        pair_1_county = county_state
      ),
    by = "pair_1_fips"
  ) |>
  left_join(
    county_lookup |>
      rename(
        pair_2_fips = county_fips,
        pair_2_county = county_state
      ),
    by = "pair_2_fips"
  ) |> 
  select(pair_1_fips, pair_1_county, pair_2_fips, pair_2_county, total_exemptions)
错误于select(left_join(left_join(county_pairs, rename(county_lookup, : 
  参数没有用(pair_1_fips, pair_1_county, pair_2_fips, pair_2_county, total_exemptions)

Result

The the county pairs with the most interactions (by number of exemptions) are shown in Table 1.

2

Determine which counties show the highest in-migration and out-migration.

Method

To identify the counties with the highest in-migration and out-migration, I aggregated migration flows at the county level using the number of exemptions as the measure of migration volume. Specifically, I summed exemptions across all records for each destination county (dest_fips) to measure total in-migration, and across all records for each origin county (orig_fips) to measure total out-migration. After ranking counties in descending order of total exemptions, I joined the results with the county lookup table to attach readable county names and state abbreviations.

# counties with the highest in-migration
top_in_migration = migration_df |>
  group_by(dest_fips) |>
  summarise(
    total_in_exemptions = sum(exemptions, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_in_exemptions)) |> 
  left_join(county_lookup, by = c("dest_fips" = "county_fips")) |> 
  select(dest_fips, county_state, total_in_exemptions)

top_in_migration |> 
  head(5) |> 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 2. Counties with the highest in-migration") |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)
Table 2. Counties with the highest in-migration
dest_fips county_state total_in_exemptions
06037 Los Angeles County, California 158722
04013 Maricopa County, Arizona 126086
06065 Riverside County, California 115246
48201 Harris County, Texas 98680
06071 San Bernardino County, California 98385 

# counties with the highest out-migration
top_out_migration = migration_df |>
  group_by(orig_fips) |>
  summarise(
    total_out_exemptions = sum(exemptions, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_out_exemptions)) |> 
  left_join(county_lookup, by = c("orig_fips" = "county_fips")) |> 
  select(orig_fips, county_state, total_out_exemptions)

top_out_migration |> 
  head(5) |> 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 3. Counties with the highest out-migration") |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE)
Table 3. Counties with the highest out-migration
orig_fips county_state total_out_exemptions
06037 Los Angeles County, California 245959
17031 Cook County, Illinois 171269
48201 Harris County, Texas 121938
06073 San Diego County, California 114115
06059 Orange County, California 112946

Result

Table 2 shows counties with the highest in-migration, and Table 3 shows counties with the highest out-migration.

3

Calculate net migration for the New York metropolitan area.

Method

I defined the New York metropolitan area using the current metropolitan county classification. I used ChatGPT to help identify the counties in the metropolitan area and their corresponding FIPS codes. Based on these county FIPS codes, I identified migration flows crossing the metropolitan boundary. Inflows were defined as flows from outside the metropolitan area into it, and outflows were defined as flows from the metropolitan area to outside it. Net migration was calculated as inflow minus outflow.

# the FIPS codes of metropolitan area counties
ny_metro_fips = c(
  "36005", # Bronx County, NY
  "36047", # Kings County, NY
  "36061", # New York County, NY
  "36081", # Queens County, NY
  "36085", # Richmond County, NY
  "36119", # Westchester County, NY
  "34003", # Bergen County, NJ
  "34017", # Hudson County, NJ
  "34031", # Passaic County, NJ
  "36087", # Rockland County, NY
  "36079", # Putnam County, NY
  "36059", # Nassau County, NY
  "36103", # Suffolk County, NY
  "34023", # Middlesex County, NJ
  "34025", # Monmouth County, NJ
  "34029", # Ocean County, NJ
  "34035", # Somerset County, NJ
  "34013", # Essex County, NJ
  "34039", # Union County, NJ
  "34027", # Morris County, NJ
  "34037", # Sussex County, NJ
  "34019"  # Hunterdon County, NJ
)

ny_metro_net_migration = migration_df |>
  # Keep only flows crossing the metro boundary:
  # 1) inflow: from outside the metro into the metro
  # 2) outflow: from the metro to outside the metro
  filter(
    (dest_fips %in% ny_metro_fips & !orig_fips %in% ny_metro_fips) |
    (orig_fips %in% ny_metro_fips & !dest_fips %in% ny_metro_fips)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    flow_type = case_when(
      dest_fips %in% ny_metro_fips & !orig_fips %in% ny_metro_fips ~ "inflow",
      orig_fips %in% ny_metro_fips & !dest_fips %in% ny_metro_fips ~ "outflow"
    )
  ) |>
  # Sum exemptions for inflows and outflows separately
  group_by(flow_type) |>
  summarise(total_exemptions = sum(exemptions, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = flow_type,
    values_from = total_exemptions,
    values_fill = 0
  ) |>
  mutate(net_migration = inflow - outflow)

ny_metro_net_migration |> 
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 4. Net migration for the New York Metropolitan Area") |> 
  kableExtra::kable_styling()
Table 4. Net migration for the New York Metropolitan Area
inflow outflow net_migration
128931 265940 -137009

Result

As shown in Table 4, the New York metropolitan area had 128,931 inflows and 265,940 outflows, resulting in a net migration of -137,009. This indicates an overall net out-migration.

4

Data Enrichment Challenge: Using publicly available GenAI tools, augment this migration data with at least three additional contextual data points (economic indicators, demographic information, etc.) that help explain the migration patterns.

migration patterns: county pair, in-flow, out-flow, net flow, flow income, personal flow income

factors: median household income, unemployment rate, Rural-Urban Continuum Code, housing rent, population, male-female ratio, young ratio

Import the contextual datasets.

income_df = read_excel(
  "contextual data/est03all.xls", skip = 1) |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    state_fips = str_pad(state_fips, width = 2, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    county_fips = str_pad(county_fips, width = 3, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    state_county_fips = str_c(state_fips, county_fips)
  ) |> 
  rename(
    income_ci_lower = x90_percent_ci_lower_bound,
    income_ci_upper = x90_percent_ci_upper_bound
  )

rent_df = read_excel("contextual data/FY2003_50Rents_County.xls") |> 
  janitor::clean_names() |> 
  mutate(
    state_fips = str_pad(state, width = 2, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    county_fips = str_pad(county, width = 3, side = "left", pad = "0"),
    state_county_fips = str_c(state_fips, county_fips))

unemp_df = read_excel("contextual data/laucnty03.xlsx", skip = 1) |> 
  janitor::clean_names() |> 
  rename(
    state_fips = state_fips_code,
    county_fips = county_fips_code,
    unemp_percent = unemployment_rate_percent
  ) |> 
  mutate(state_county_fips = str_c(state_fips, county_fips))
  
rucc_df = read_excel("contextual data/ruralurbancodes2003.xls") |> 
  janitor::clean_names() |> 
  select(-x1993_rural_urban_continuum_code) |>
  rename(
    rucc = x2003_rural_urban_continuum_code,
    population = x2000_population
  )

pop_est = read_csv(
  "contextual data/population_est.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  mutate(
    state = stringr::str_pad(as.character(state), 2, pad = "0"),
    county = stringr::str_pad(as.character(county), 3, pad = "0"),
    county_fips = paste0(state, county)
  ) |>
  group_by(county_fips, stname, ctyname) |>
  summarise(
    sex_ratio =
      first(popestimate2003[sex == 1 & agegrp == 0]) /
      first(popestimate2003[sex == 2 & agegrp == 0]),
    young_share =
      sum(popestimate2003[sex == 0 & agegrp %in% 5:7]) /
      first(popestimate2003[sex == 0 & agegrp == 0]),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  select(county_fips, sex_ratio, young_share)

Factors affecting gross migration

Calculate gross migration for each county.

gross_flow = full_join(
  top_out_migration,
  top_in_migration,
  by = c("orig_fips" = "dest_fips"),
  suffix = c("_out", "_in")
) |>
  mutate(
    county_fips = orig_fips,
    county_state = coalesce(county_state_out, county_state_in),
    total_out_exemptions = replace_na(total_out_exemptions, 0),
    total_in_exemptions = replace_na(total_in_exemptions, 0),
    gross_flow = total_out_exemptions + total_in_exemptions
  ) |> 
  select(county_fips, county_state, total_out_exemptions, total_in_exemptions, gross_flow) |> 
  arrange(desc(gross_flow))

Join the factors table on the gross migration table.

gross_flow_joined = gross_flow |>
  left_join(
    income_df |>
      select(
        state_county_fips,
        median_household_income
      ),
    by = c("county_fips" = "state_county_fips")
  ) |>
  left_join(
    unemp_df |>
      select(
        state_county_fips,
        unemp_percent
      ),
    by = c("county_fips" = "state_county_fips")
  ) |>
  left_join(
    rucc_df |>
      select(
        fips_code,
        population,
        rucc
      ),
    by = c("county_fips" = "fips_code")
  ) |>
  left_join(
    rent_df |>
      select(
        state_county_fips,
        rent50_2
      ),
    by = c("county_fips" = "state_county_fips")
  ) |> 
  left_join(
    pop_est,
    by = "county_fips"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    across(
      c(
        median_household_income,
        unemp_percent,
        population,
        rucc,
        rent50_2
      ),
      ~ readr::parse_number(as.character(.x))
    ),
    gross_flow_rate = gross_flow / population * 1000
  )

EDA

Distribution of gross flow:

ggplot(gross_flow_joined, aes(x = population, y = gross_flow)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  theme_bw()

Gross flow is highly correlated with population, so we use gross flow rate instead.

Distribution of gross flow rate:

ggplot(gross_flow_joined, aes(x = gross_flow_rate)) +
  geom_density(fill = "grey") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Density of Gross Flow Rate",
    x = "Gross flow (per 1000)",
    y = "Density"
  )

Distribution of the continuous variables:

eda_long = gross_flow_joined |>
  select(
    median_household_income,
    unemp_percent,
    rent50_2,
    population,
    sex_ratio,
    young_share
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value"
  )

ggplot(eda_long, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_density(fill = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Density",
    title = "Distributions of raw continuous variables"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Distribution of the categorical variable:

ggplot(gross_flow_joined, aes(x = as.factor(rucc))) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of RUCC",
    x = "RUCC",
    y = "Count"
  )

According to the EDA results, some variables distributions are skewed and need to be taken logarithm.

analysis_df = 
  gross_flow_joined |> 
  mutate(
      rucc = factor(rucc),
      log_income = log(median_household_income),
      log_rent = log(rent50_2),
      log_population = log(population)
    )

# density plot after taking log
eda_long_2 = analysis_df |>
  select(
    log_income,
    log_rent,
    log_population
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value"
  )

ggplot(eda_long_2, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_density(fill = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ variable, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Density",
    title = "Distributions of adjusted continuous variables"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Find out the relationship between gross flow and the predictors.

analysis_df |>
  select(gross_flow_rate, log_income, unemp_percent, log_rent, log_population, sex_ratio, young_share) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(log_income, unemp_percent, log_rent, log_population, sex_ratio, young_share),
    names_to = "predictor",
    values_to = "x"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = gross_flow_rate)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~predictor, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_bw()

y_upper = quantile(analysis_df$gross_flow_rate, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE)

ggplot(analysis_df, aes(x = factor(rucc), y = gross_flow_rate)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) + 
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.2, size = 0.8) + 
  coord_cartesian( 
    ylim = c(min(analysis_df$gross_flow_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
             y_upper) ) + 
  theme_bw() + 
  labs( title = "Gross Flow by RUCC", 
        x = "RUCC", 
        y = "Gross Flow Rate" )

Based on the exploratory plots, I decided to retain log_income, log_population, log_rent, young_share, and RUCC for the model. These variables all showed at least some visible relationship with gross flow rate and are also substantively relevant to migration patterns. In particular, log_income, log_population, and young_share showed clearer positive trends, while log_rent and RUCC still appeared informative enough to keep for further modeling.

I decided to remove sex_ratio and unemp_percent from the main model. The scatterplots suggested that sex_ratio had little clear association with gross flow rate, and unemp_percent also showed only a weak and inconsistent pattern. Since both variables appeared to contribute limited explanatory value at the EDA stage, they were excluded to keep the model more focused and interpretable.

Correlation Analysis

cor_mat = analysis_df |>
  select(
    log_income,
    rucc,
    log_rent,
    log_population,
    young_share
  ) |>
  drop_na() |>
  mutate(
    rucc = as.numeric(as.character(rucc))
  ) |>
  cor(method = "spearman")

cor_long = as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat))

ggplot(cor_long, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Freq, 2)), size = 4) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#2166ac",
    mid = "white",
    high = "#b2182b",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-1, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Spearman\ncorrelation",
    title = "Correlation among analysis variables"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

Based on the correlation analysis, log_population was excluded from the final model because it showed the strongest correlation with another predictor, rucc (Spearman ρ = -0.74). This suggested potential redundancy, so I retained log_income, rucc, log_rent, and young_share for modeling.

Fit model and do VIF analysis:

fit_gam_nb = mgcv::gam(
  gross_flow ~ log_income + rucc +
    s(log_rent) +
    s(young_share) +
    offset(log(population)),
  family = nb(),
  data = count_df,
  method = "REML"
)

summary(fit_gam_nb)

Family: Negative Binomial(5.166) 
Link function: log 

Formula:
gross_flow ~ log_income + rucc + s(log_rent) + s(young_share) + 
    offset(log(population))

Parametric coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -9.75535    0.54315 -17.961  < 2e-16 ***
log_income   0.67608    0.05106  13.241  < 2e-16 ***
rucc2       -0.13774    0.03445  -3.999 6.37e-05 ***
rucc3       -0.17188    0.03615  -4.755 1.99e-06 ***
rucc4       -0.29038    0.04080  -7.117 1.10e-12 ***
rucc5       -0.44611    0.05193  -8.591  < 2e-16 ***
rucc6       -0.29629    0.03596  -8.238  < 2e-16 ***
rucc7       -0.55433    0.03773 -14.692  < 2e-16 ***
rucc8       -0.31358    0.04464  -7.024 2.16e-12 ***
rucc9       -0.69550    0.04348 -15.997  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

Approximate significance of smooth terms:
                 edf Ref.df Chi.sq p-value    
s(log_rent)    4.882  5.989   21.6 0.00154 ** 
s(young_share) 7.164  8.223  153.0 < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1

R-sq.(adj) =  0.702   Deviance explained =   41%
